title: 💵 Form 1040-ES vs Withholding: Which Saves Money
description:
When to use Form 1040-ES quarterly payments versus W-2 withholding adjustments to manage your tax bill in 2026. Plus the UK Payment on Account system explained.

Personal finance, estimated taxes, 1040-ES, withholding, freelancer taxes 2026.

segments.voice: Most freelancers and side hustlers learn about Form 1040-ES the hard way, by getting hit with an underpayment penalty in April. The IRS expects tax to be paid as you earn it, not all at once on tax day. Today we walk through quarterly estimated tax payments, when to use them, and when to adjust W-2 withholding instead.

The basic rule. Per IRC section 6654, you must pay either ninety percent of your current year tax liability or one hundred percent of last year's liability, one hundred ten percent if your AGI was over one hundred fifty thousand. Pay either of those through any combination of withholding and quarterly payments and you avoid the underpayment penalty.

Who needs 1040-ES. Anyone who expects to owe more than one thousand dollars in tax after subtracting withholding and credits. So a freelancer with no W-2 income definitely needs it. A side hustler with a day job whose W-2 withholding does not cover the side income tax liability also needs it, unless they bump up withholding.

The dates. Quarterly payment deadlines are April fifteenth, June fifteenth, September fifteenth, and January fifteenth of the following year. Note the second quarter is only two months. The first quarter is January through March, second is April through May, third is June through August, fourth is September through December.

How to calculate. The simplest method is the prior-year safe harbor. Take last year's total tax bill, divide by four, send that amount each quarter. Done. The IRS will not penalize you regardless of what your current year ends up looking like, because you met the safe harbor by paying one hundred percent of last year's liability.

When to use the current year method. If your income is dropping significantly, the prior year method overpays, and you get a big refund the following spring. The IRS does not pay interest on most refunds for the holding period. So if you can reasonably project current-year tax, paying ninety percent of that is more efficient.

How to actually pay. The IRS website lets you pay directly from your bank account at irs.gov slash payments using IRS Direct Pay, no fees, no account creation required. Just enter your information, the amount, and confirm. The system processes within one business day. EFTPS is another option that lets you schedule payments in advance, useful for the disciplined.

The withholding alternative. If you have W-2 income from a day job, you can avoid quarterly payments entirely by increasing W-2 withholding to cover the expected total tax. File a new W-4 with your employer adding extra withholding on line 4(c). The benefit, withholding is treated as paid evenly throughout the year regardless of when in the year it actually happened.

The withholding hack. If you forgot to make estimated payments and it is December, you can dramatically increase your last paycheck withholding to catch up. Withholding is treated as paid evenly across the year per IRC section 6654, so a December withholding bump retroactively cures earlier quarter shortfalls. Quarterly payments do not have this benefit.

Self-employment tax in the calculation. If you are self-employed, your quarterly payments need to cover self-employment tax, fifteen point three percent on net earnings, plus regular income tax. Most people forget the SE tax piece and underpay. The Form 1040-ES worksheet walks through the combined calculation, but most freelancers find tax software faster.

Common quarterly mistakes. Paying the same amount each quarter when income is uneven, missing the second quarter because it sneaks up only two months after the first, paying based on gross instead of net of expenses and deductions, forgetting state quarterly payments which run on a separate schedule in most states.

State quarterly payments. Most states with income tax also require quarterly estimated payments. The deadlines usually mirror federal but the calculation is separate. California has its own Form 540-ES, New York has IT-2105, Massachusetts has Form 1-ES. Each state department of revenue website explains the rules and provides electronic payment options.

What happens if you skip a quarter. The underpayment penalty kicks in at roughly eight percent annualized per the Federal Reserve update of October two thousand twenty-five. The penalty is calculated quarter by quarter, so missing the second quarter and catching up in the third still creates a penalty for the missed quarter. The IRS Form 2210 calculates this exactly.

Penalty waivers. The IRS waives the underpayment penalty in three situations. First, if you became disabled or had a casualty loss. Second, if your prior year AGI was below the safe harbor and you had no prior year tax liability. Third, the first year you switched from W-2 to self-employment, the IRS sometimes grants leniency on Form 2210 line eight.

The annualized income method. If your income comes in lumps, like a writer who gets advances or a consultant with one big quarterly project, the annualized income installment method on Schedule AI lets you base each quarter's payment on actual income earned to that point, not on equal projections. This avoids overpaying early when income is back-loaded.

Example for the annualized method. A consultant earned five thousand in quarter one, eight thousand in quarter two, and forty thousand in quarter four. Equal estimated payments would have overpaid quarters one and two and underpaid quarter four. The annualized method matches each payment to the actual cumulative income, eliminating the underpayment penalty for the unevenly earned year.

UK readers, your Payment on Account system. Once your Self Assessment tax bill exceeds one thousand pounds, HMRC requires payments on account in addition to your normal tax. Each year you pay fifty percent of the prior year's tax bill on January thirty-first and another fifty percent on July thirty-first, on top of any balancing payment for the current year.

How payment on account works in practice. Year one tax bill of two thousand pounds. Year two requires the full year two tax plus two payments on account of one thousand each, totaling four thousand. This creates a brutal first-year cash flow shock for new self-employed UK filers. Plan for it from day one or face a very rough January.
segments.visual: UK first year self employed shock

segments.voice: Reducing UK payment on account. If you expect your current year income to be lower, you can request a reduction in your payments on account through HMRC online. The risk, if your actual liability turns out higher than your reduced payments, you owe interest on the under-payment. Be honest in your projection or accept the cash flow hit.
segments.visual: UK reduce payment on account request

segments.voice: Best practice for US freelancers. Open a separate tax savings account with your business bank. Every time a client invoice gets paid, transfer twenty-five to thirty percent into the tax account. When quarterly payments hit, the money is already there. The discipline of treating the tax bucket as untouchable is the single best behavioral fix for new freelancers.

Software that calculates for you. QuickBooks Self-Employed estimates your quarterly payments based on actual income and expenses. FreshBooks does the same. TurboTax has a built-in calculator. The free option, the IRS Tax Withholding Estimator at irs.gov, walks you through the calculation in about ten minutes for both W-2 and self-employed income.

Adjusting mid-year. Income surge in quarter three? Recalculate your estimated payments for quarters three and four. Income drop? Recalculate too. The system is designed to be flexible. Just keep a record of your projection rationale in case the IRS questions a quarterly underpayment later. Documentation always wins in tax disputes.

When 1040-ES does not apply. If you expect your total tax for the year to be under one thousand dollars after withholding and credits, no estimated payments required. Most students with part-time income and most retirees with only Social Security and modest investment income fall into this category. The IRS interactive tool confirms whether you need to file 1040-ES.

Subscribe to FinanzasClara and hit the bell. What's coming next week, the exact spreadsheet a freelance writer uses to manage her quarterly tax payments and how she paid zero penalties despite a wildly uneven income year. Drop a comment with the quarter you find hardest to fund and I will share the cash flow strategy in a follow-up.
